question: Based on Table 5.4-1 and Figure 5.4-1, is the NuScale SG design adequate for transferring the full thermal power from primary to secondary?

nuscale_ch05_0025.title: References
nuscale_ch05_0025.summary: This section details the materials used in the NuScale pressurizer, including the RPV upper shell, baffle plate, heater bundles, and associated components. It specifies the use of low alloy steel, various grades of stainless steel, and nickel-based alloys, all in accordance with ASME BPVC Section II standards, to ensure corrosion resistance and material compatibility within the reactor coolant system.

nuscale_ch05_0020.title: Steam Generators
nuscale_ch05_0020.summary: Each NuScale Power Module’s reactor pressure vessel contains two integral steam generators, which are part of the reactor coolant system and share the vessel’s pressure boundary. The steam generator system includes feedwater and main steam piping, thermal relief valves, and internal plenums, with the reactor pressure vessel itself forming the shell and pressure boundary for the steam generators.

nuscale_ch05_0019.title: Reactor Coolant System Component and Subsystem Design
nuscale_ch05_0019.summary: This section describes the design of the NuScale Power Module’s Reactor Coolant System (RCS) components and subsystems, including the reactor pressure vessel, steam generators, safety and emergency valves, and associated piping. It outlines the integration of these components within the RPV, highlights the boundaries between primary and secondary systems, and references where detailed design bases for specific components can be found in other sections of the report.

nuscale_ch05_0002.title: Summary Description
nuscale_ch05_0002.summary: The NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) uses natural circulation, eliminating the need for pumps or external piping, and is integrated within each NuScale Power Module. The RCS is designed to safely transfer heat, maintain proper coolant chemistry, and ensure uniform boron concentration, supporting both normal and off-normal plant operations. These features collectively ensure the system meets performance and safety requirements for each module.

nuscale_ch05_0016.title: Pressure-Temperature Limits, Pressurized Thermal Shock, and Charpy Upper-Shelf Energy Data and Analyses
nuscale_ch05_0016.summary: This section outlines the methodology and regulatory compliance for establishing pressure-temperature limits for the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) over the plant’s 60-year lifespan. It details how these limits are calculated using ASME and NRC guidelines, accounting for vessel embrittlement, and provides the corresponding curves and values to ensure protection against non-ductile failure.

nuscale_ch05_0022.title: Decay Heat Removal System
nuscale_ch05_0022.summary: The Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) is an engineered safety feature designed to remove residual and decay heat from the reactor core during non-LOCA events when normal cooling is unavailable, ensuring safe shutdown without external power. It maintains the reactor coolant system temperature below 420°F within 36 hours, protecting fuel and pressure boundaries, and its reliability and risk significance are evaluated through established programs. The DHRS operates independently of the Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS), with both systems able to function together if needed.

nuscale_ch05_0012.title: Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary Leakage Detection
nuscale_ch05_0012.summary: The NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) does not use traditional components with designed leakage rates, making it difficult to distinguish between identified and unidentified leaks inside containment using current instrumentation. All leakage into containment is treated as unidentified until the source is determined, with leakage rates monitored through RCS inventory balance and containment evacuation system (CES) flow rates, and operational limits set in technical specifications. The leakage detection systems are designed to be reliable, redundant, and sensitive enough to support safety analyses.

nuscale_ch05_0003.title: Design Basis
nuscale_ch05_0003.summary: The Design Basis section outlines that the NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) uses natural circulation without pumps or external piping, ensuring safe and efficient heat transfer from the reactor core to the steam generator. The RCS is designed to maintain fuel integrity, minimize solid deposits, and ensure uniform boron concentration under all operating conditions, supporting both performance and safety requirements.

nuscale_ch05_0024.title: Pressurizer
nuscale_ch05_0024.summary: The pressurizer, integrated into the upper region of the reactor vessel and separated by a baffle plate, regulates reactor coolant system (RCS) pressure by maintaining a saturated steam-water interface. It uses heaters and spray flow to control pressure and is designed to handle operating transients without triggering reactor trips or safety system actuations, while providing continuous monitoring and control through instrumentation.

nuscale_ch05_0005.title: System Components
nuscale_ch05_0005.summary: This section describes key components of the NuScale reactor system. It details the Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV), which houses the reactor core and supports major systems, and outlines the main Reactor Coolant System (RCS) piping and its connections. The section also introduces the pressurizer (PZR), which is integrated into the RPV, and summarizes their roles in maintaining system integrity and safety.

nuscale_ch05_0009.title: Overpressure Protection
nuscale_ch05_0009.summary: This section outlines NuScale's compliance with overpressure protection requirements, referencing relevant ASME codes and NRC regulations (10 CFR 50.55a). It details how code cases for design, fabrication, inspection, and testing are selected and verified to meet regulatory guidance, with specific code cases consolidated in Table 5.2-1.

nuscale_ch05_0014.title: Reactor Vessel
nuscale_ch05_0014.summary: The Reactor Vessel section describes the NuScale Power Module's reactor pressure vessel (RPV), which houses the reactor core, steam generators, and pressurizer within a single vessel surrounded by a containment vessel. The RPV serves as a critical barrier to fission product release and supports various reactor components, with its materials and fabrication adhering to stringent ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code standards to ensure long-term integrity and safety.

nuscale_ch05_0006.title: System Evaluation
nuscale_ch05_0006.summary: The System Evaluation section describes how the NuScale Power Plant optimizes performance across various power levels by establishing steady-state parameters such as coolant temperatures, flow rates, and pressures, using both software and manual calculations. It emphasizes maximizing electrical generation, efficient use of support systems, and maintaining safety margins, while accounting for uncertainties in flow resistance and system design. The design supports natural circulation with low pressure losses, and uncertainties like SG tube plugging do not affect primary coolant flow rate determination.

edges.description: Section A describes the materials and components of the pressurizer, which is part of the RPV assembly detailed in Section B, where the steam generators are also described as integral components of the same system, thus both sections provide structural details about different but related subsystems within the overall reactor coolant system.

Section A provides detailed material specifications for the pressurizer components, which are part of the broader system components generally described in Section B, including the steam generators relevant to the analysis question.
Section A provides detailed material specifications for the pressurizer components, which are part of the RCS described in Section B's general summary of system components and functions.

Section A references material specifications and component descriptions that are further detailed in Section B, which provides regulatory compliance and examination requirements for those materials and attachments.

Section A provides detailed component-level design information about the steam generators within the NuScale Power Module, while Section B offers a general summary of the reactor coolant system that includes the steam generators as part of its overall configuration.

Section A describes the components and design basis of the NuScale steam generators relevant to heat transfer, while Section B discusses pressure-temperature limits and material requirements for the reactor vessel, which set the boundary conditions that the steam generator design must operate within to ensure safe and adequate heat transfer.

Section A provides specific component-level design details of the reactor coolant system, including the steam generators, which are generally described as part of the overall system in Section B, thus Section A specifies the details for the general system overview in Section B.

Section A provides a general overview of the reactor coolant system's design and function, while Section B details specific ASME code compliance for reactor vessel attachments, with both sections connected through their focus on the structural and operational integrity of the reactor vessel as part of the overall system.

Section A describes the design basis and function of the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS), which relies on the steam generators (SGs) described in Section B as part of the integrated reactor coolant system to transfer heat from the primary to the secondary side, thus connecting the adequacy of SG design to the DHRS's ability to meet its cooling requirements.

Section A describes the design basis and function of the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS), which relies on the steam generators (SGs) described in Section B as integral components within the reactor coolant system for transferring heat from the primary to the secondary side.

Section B provides references to material descriptions in Section A, linking the pressurizer component materials to the integrity and leakage detection requirements of the reactor coolant pressure boundary.

Section A discusses leakage detection in the reactor coolant pressure boundary, which is relevant to Section B's description of the steam generators as part of the reactor coolant system, since undetected leakage could affect the SG's ability to transfer thermal power as required by the analysis question.

Section A discusses leakage detection capabilities of the reactor coolant pressure boundary, while Section B describes the physical components and design of the reactor coolant system, including the steam generators; thus, Section B provides the general system context that Section A's leakage detection applies to.

Section A provides a general overview of the reactor coolant system's design and function, while Section B specifies detailed material and component information for the pressurizer, which is a part of the system described in Section A.

Section A provides a general overview of the reactor coolant system's reliance on natural circulation, while Section B specifies that the steam generators are integral components within the reactor pressure vessel, directly connecting the RCS design basis to the SGs' role in heat transfer.

Section A provides a general overview of the reactor coolant system's reliance on natural circulation, while Section B specifies the detailed components of the system, including the steam generators relevant to heat transfer adequacy.
Section B provides references to other sections that describe the materials and components mentioned in Section A's general description of the pressurizer design.

Section A describes the pressurizer's function and integration within the reactor vessel, while Section B lists the steam generators as co-located components within the same reactor pressure vessel, establishing that both are part of the overall reactor coolant system design but without directly connecting their roles in thermal power transfer.

Section A describes the pressurizer as a component within the reactor vessel, while Section B lists the pressurizer (along with the steam generators) as part of the overall reactor coolant system, establishing that the pressurizer is one of several integrated components relevant to the system's thermal power transfer function.

Section A describes the Decay Heat Removal System's design basis for removing residual and decay heat when normal secondary-side cooling is unavailable, while Section B provides a general summary of the Reactor Coolant System, which is the primary system from which heat must be transferred, thus Section A specifies how heat removal is achieved for the system described in Section B.

Section A describes the Decay Heat Removal System's design basis for removing residual and decay heat from the reactor coolant system, while Section B details the pressurizer's function in maintaining system pressure, with both sections connected by their roles in ensuring safe thermal and pressure management within the reactor coolant system.

Section A discusses the inability to distinguish certain types of leakage in the RCS due to the absence of traditional components, while Section B explains that the NuScale RCS design omits such components (e.g., pumps, external piping), directly connecting the design features to the operational limitations described in Section A.

Section B describes the design and function of the pressurizer as part of the reactor coolant system, while Section A discusses leakage detection challenges within the same system, connecting them through their focus on the integrity and monitoring of the reactor coolant pressure boundary.

Section A provides a general overview of the reactor coolant system, stating that the pressurizer is an integral component, while Section B gives specific design details about the pressurizer's configuration and its integration with the RCS, thus specifying the general description from Section A.

Section A describes operational procedures involving the steam generators (SGs) during specific plant conditions, while Section B provides the design details and configuration of the SGs, connecting them by showing how the operational use of SGs (A) depends on their design and capabilities as specified in (B).

Section B provides a general description of the reactor coolant system and its components, including the steam generators (SGs), while Section A specifies operational procedures and conditions for those components, such as isolation and water chemistry management.
Section B references Section A for material specifications of pressurizer components, indicating that the detailed material information in Section A supports the component descriptions in Section B.

Section A outlines the overpressure protection and code compliance requirements that the steam generator design in Section B must satisfy to ensure safe operation, connecting them through regulatory prerequisites for system adequacy.

Section A outlines the overpressure protection and code compliance requirements that must be met by the reactor coolant boundary, while Section B describes the design and components of the reactor coolant system, including the steam generators whose adequacy for heat transfer is under analysis, thus Section A's requirements set the regulatory framework that Section B's design must satisfy.

Section A provides a general description of the reactor vessel and its components, while Section B specifies detailed material and design information for the pressurizer, which is a component described in Section A.

Section A describes the overall configuration of the NuScale Power Module, noting that it contains two steam generators within the reactor pressure vessel, while Section B provides further detail on the steam generators as integral components of the reactor coolant system, thus Section B specifies the components that Section A generally describes.

Section A provides a general description of the reactor vessel and its components, while Section B specifies the detailed design and arrangement of those components, including the steam generators relevant to the thermal power transfer analysis.
Section A references Section B for further description of the steam generators (SGs), indicating that details relevant to the system evaluation in Section A can be found in Section B.

Section A references Section B for further details on the steam generators (SGs), indicating that the system evaluation in Section A relies on the component and subsystem design information provided in Section B.

Section A references Section B for further details on the steam generators (SGs), indicating that the system evaluation in Section A relies on the component and subsystem design information provided in Section B.

Section A describes operational procedures and system component interactions relevant to the steam generators (SGs), while Section B outlines the regulatory codes and standards (ASME BPVC, 10 CFR 50.55a) that the design and operation of these components, including SGs, must comply with, thus connecting operational adequacy to regulatory compliance.

Section A describes operational procedures and system components, including the steam generators (SGs), while Section B provides the physical configuration and integration of the SGs within the reactor vessel, connecting the operational context to the design layout relevant for assessing heat transfer adequacy.

Section A describes the operational steps and system components involved in isolating and preparing the steam generators (SGs), while Section B evaluates the SGs' performance in transferring heat from primary to secondary side, referencing the SGs described in Section A.

Section A outlines the overpressure protection and code compliance requirements for reactor coolant boundary components, which include the reactor vessel described in Section B as part of the NuScale Power Module, thus connecting them through regulatory and design standards that ensure the integrity and safety of the reactor vessel.

Section A outlines compliance with ASME and NRC overpressure protection codes for the reactor coolant boundary, while Section B discusses the system evaluation criteria for the steam generators (SGs), with both sections connected through the requirement that SG design and operation must adhere to regulatory and code-based safety standards.
Section B discusses the evaluation criteria and performance parameters for the steam generators (SGs), which are components described structurally in Section A as part of the reactor vessel system.
